instructor.name: Teresa Leyk
instructor.office_hours: See the Course Resources on Canvas

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Data Structures and Algorithms. (3-2). Credit 4. Specification and implementation of basic abstract data types and their associated algorithms including stacks, queues, lists, sorting and selection, searching, graphs, and hashing; performance tradeoffs of different implementations and asymptotic analysis of running time and memory usage; includes the execution of student programs written in C++.
Prerequisite: Grade C or better in CSCE 120 or CSCE 121; grade of C or better in CSCE 222 or ECEN 222, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade C or better in CSCE 120 or CSCE 121; grade of C or better in CSCE 222/ECEN 222 or ECEN 222/CSCE 222, or concurrent enrollment.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Design and implement a variety of data structures that support efficient data manipulation using the C++ programming language.
• Apply Big-O asymptotic notation to analyze algorithmic efficiency and select appropriate algorithms based on runtime and memory considerations.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Data Structures and Algorithm Analysis in C++
ISBN: 978-0132847377
Authors: Mark A. Weiss
Publisher: Pearson
Publication Date: June 13, 2013
Edition: 4th edition

chunks.content:

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Grading Criteria
| Score | Grade |
|---|---|
| 90-100 | A |
| 80-89.9 | B |
| 70-79.9 | C |
| 60-69.9 | D |
| 0-59.9 | F |

Grade Weights
| Component | Weight |
|---|---|
| Homework Assignments | 9% |
| Programming Assignments | 24% |
| Lab Activities | 10% |
| Quizzes | 12% |
| Midterm Exam 1 | 15% |
| Midterm Exam 2 | 15% |
| Final Exam | 15% |

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• Make-up exams and quizzes will only be given with documented University-approved excuses, see University Regulations. The student Rule 07 explains policies and excused absences.
• A homework assignment will be accepted up to 1 day late with a 5% penalty. Once solutions have been discussed or handed out the assignments will not be accepted.
• Please discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor.

chunks.title: Additional Course Information (Grade Determination & Appeals)
chunks.content:
• Your final grade will be determined based on written homework, programming assignments, lab activities, quizzes, and exams.
• The assignment handout will provide points distribution.
• All grade appeals must be made no later than one week after the grade is posted.

chunks.title: Additional Course Information (Attendance)
chunks.content:
• The students lab attendance is required and will be taken on regular basis. 2 bonus points will be added to your final score if you have a perfect lab attendance, or 1 point if you have only one lab absence.
• The lecture attendance will be taken by pop quizzes. The students are responsible to learn all material covered in class, read the assigned text from the textbook and do homework assignments.

chunks.title: Additional Course Information (Assignments & Quizzes)
chunks.content:
• All homework assignments will be announced in class and posted on Canvas. See the link to the course calendar on Canvas for deadlines.
• The written part of homework assignments or programming reports, and the Cover Page should be typed preferably using LaTeX or LaTeX-based apps, for instance Overleaf (an online LaTeX text editor). The homework in PDF format should be submitted to Gradescope.
• All programming assignments should be implemented in C++, compiled and run on a computer (preferably using Linux), and transferred to Gradescope for grading.
• Quizzes are over material covered during the lectures and/or assigned reading from the course material.
• The Instructor and/or TAs can request oral exam to check student's knowledge over material covered by test, homework, assignment or lecture and lab activities.

chunks.title: Additional Course Information (Canvas Usage)
chunks.content: • Use Canvas to get access to the lecture notes, quizzes and tests, assignments, and check your grades on the regular basis during the semester.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves, although discussion with TAs, PTs and Instructor is allowed. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and/or using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT. (Adapted from University of Massachusetts Amherst)
